prompt: circuit with two LEDs, each connected through 220 ohm resistors to separate digital pins, plus one pushbutton input with proper resistor wiring, ground and 5V rails connected correctly,

trace.text:
I'll build this circuit in one shot. Let me plan it first:

**Plan:**
- LED1 (red) → 220Ω R1 → GND, driven by D12
- LED2 (green) → 220Ω R2 → GND, driven by D13
- Button on D2 with 10kΩ pull-down resistor (one leg → 5V, other leg → D2 + resistor → GND)

toolResult.layout:

  [2] LED2 (led) → row=3 col=2 id=40a9aa16-779b-40f9-81b0-a335aa0e2ae5
  [3] R2 (resistor) → row=4 col=3 id=116e14e9-8702-405b-8941-0bf8c65ac010

trace.text:
Circuit built successfully! ✅ Here's the full breakdown:

---

## 🔌 Wiring Table

| Arduino Pin | Wire Color | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D12** | Red | LED1 (Red) Anode | LED1 output |
| GND | Black | R1 (220Ω) → LED1 Cathode | LED1 current limit |
| **D13** | Green | LED2 (Green) Anode | LED2 output |
| GND | Black | R2 (220Ω) → LED2 Cathode | LED2 current limit |
| **5V** | Red | BTN1 Pin 1 | Button power rail |
| **D2** | Yellow | BTN1 Pin 2 (signal out) | Button input |
| **D2** | Yellow | R3 (10kΩ) Pin 1 | Pull-down tie point |
| **GND** | Black | R3 (10kΩ) Pin 2 | Pull-down to ground |

---

## ⚡ Power Budget Summary

| Pin | Load | Current | Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| D12 | LED1 + R1 (220Ω) | (5V−2V)/220Ω ≈ **~13.6 mA** | ✅ < 20mA |
| D13 | LED2 + R2 (220Ω) | (5V−2V)/220Ω ≈ **~13.6 mA** | ✅ < 20mA |
| D2 | Button + 10kΩ pull-down | 5V/10kΩ ≈ **~0.5 mA** | ✅ negligible |
| **Total Board** | All active loads | **~27.7 mA** | ✅ well under 200mA Uno limit |

> 🟢 All per-pin currents are safely below the 20mA limit. Total draw is well within the Arduino Uno's 200mA USB power budget.

---

## 🎮 Sketch Behavior

| Condition | LED1 (Red) | LED2 (Green) |
|---|---|---|
| **Button released** | Alternating blink (400ms each) | Alternating blink (400ms each) |
| **Button pressed** | 💡 ON (solid) | 💡 ON (solid) |

- The **10kΩ pull-down** on D2 ensures a clean `LOW` when the button is not pressed — no floating pin issues.
- Serial monitor (`9600 baud`) prints state changes for easy debugging.
